question: In discussion 87, Jabu debated: Why do scientists send robotic machines like a robotic spacecraft to Mars instead of humans right now?

subtopic: Movements of the Earth (rotation and revolution)
question: In project 93, Jabu described the Earth's full revolution around the Sun. How long does it take, and what does this movement, combined with its tilted axis, cause?
correct_answer: It takes 365 and a quarter days (1 year) and causes the seasons.
wrong_answers_pool: It takes 1 year and causes eclipses only.
It takes 24 hours and causes day and night.
It takes 10 years and causes climate change.
It takes 7 days and causes the tides.
It takes 24 hours and causes the seasons.
It takes 365 days and causes earthquakes.
explanation: The yearly orbit combined with the tilt of the Earth's axis causes seasons.

question: For question 73, Piet had to name the movement of the Earth in its path around the Sun. What is it?

subtopic: Systems to explore the Moon and Mars (rovers)
question: Lerato researched for paper 19: How do scientists on Earth control a Moon rover that is millions of kilometers away on Mars?
correct_answer: By sending complex computer commands via radio signals, which take several minutes to travel there.
wrong_answers_pool: The robot is entirely controlled by aliens.
By controlling it in real-time with a joystick like a video game.
By sending signals through the ocean.
Using a very long electrical wire.
By shouting through a giant megaphone.
explanation: Due to the vast distance, radio signals take time to travel between Earth and Mars, so rovers execute programmed sequences.

subtopic: Movements of the Moon (rotation and revolution)
question: In chart 80, Johan tracked the Moon. How long does it take for the Moon to revolve once around the Earth?

subtopic: Movements of the Earth (rotation and revolution)
question: In test 21, Lerato was asked: What do we call the spinning movement of the Earth on its own axis?

explanation: Earth rotates (spins) on its axis, causing day and night.

subtopic: Systems for looking into space (telescopes)
question: Sarah read article 65 about the Hubble Space Telescope. Why do astronomers place such telescopes in orbit around Earth?
correct_answer: To get a clear view without the Earth's atmosphere blurring the images.
wrong_answers_pool: Because it is cheaper to put them in space.
Because clouds on Jupiter block the view from Earth.
Because they need to be closer to the Sun.
Because it is too cold on Earth.
Because the Moon gets in the way on Earth.
Because there is no gravity in space.
explanation: Space telescopes bypass the blurring and light absorption caused by Earth's atmosphere.

subtopic: Systems for looking into space (telescopes)
question: In geography quiz 3, Lisa was asked: The SALT (Southern African Large Telescope) is one of the largest single optical telescopes. Where is it located to ensure dark, clear skies?
correct_answer: In the Karoo near Sutherland, South Africa
wrong_answers_pool: On top of Table Mountain in Cape Town
At the bottom of the ocean
explanation: SALT is located in Sutherland because the Karoo has clear, dark, unpolluted skies.

subtopic: Movements of the Moon (rotation and revolution)
question: Lerato wondered in entry 47: Why do we always see the same side of the Moon from Earth?
correct_answer: Because the Moon's rotation time is exactly the same as its revolution time around Earth.
wrong_answers_pool: Because the Earth is spinning too fast.
Because the Earth stops the Moon from spinning.
Because the Sun's gravity locks it in place.
Because the dark side of the Moon has no light.
Because clouds always cover the other side.
explanation: Tidal locking means the Moon spins on its axis at the same rate it orbits Earth.

subtopic: Systems for looking into space (telescopes)
question: Thabo explained how a large optical telescope like optical telescope works to see stars (Presentation 56). What is the correct method?
correct_answer: It uses curved mirrors or lenses to gather and focus light from distant objects.
wrong_answers_pool: It shoots a laser beam to illuminate the stars.
It measures the wind speed in space.
It uses a giant magnifying glass only.
It uses magnets to pull the image closer.
It sends out radio waves and waits for an echo.
It relies on x-rays from the sun.
explanation: Optical telescopes gather visible light using precision optics.

question: Zanele wondered in entry 27: Why do we always see the same side of the Moon from Earth?
correct_answer: Because the Moon's rotation time is exactly the same as its revolution time around Earth.

Because the Sun's gravity locks it in place.
Because the Earth stops the Moon from spinning.
explanation: Tidal locking means the Moon spins on its axis at the same rate it orbits Earth.

explanation: The Sun is a star at the center of our Solar System, and all 8 planets orbit it.

subtopic: The Solar System
question: For essay 55, Jabu explained why Venus stays in its orbit around the Sun instead of flying off into space. What is the reason?
correct_answer: The strong force of gravity from the Sun pulls it inward, keeping it in orbit.
wrong_answers_pool: It is pushed by the solar wind.
The Moon's gravity holds it in place.
It is magnetic and sticks to the Sun.
It is stuck in a giant cloud of gas.
It is attached by an invisible string.
explanation: Gravity is the attractive force that keeps celestial bodies in their orbits.

subtopic: Movements of the Moon (rotation and revolution)
question: In chart 46, Jabu tracked the Moon. How long does it take for the Moon to revolve once around the Earth?
correct_answer: About 27 to 29 days (roughly a month)
explanation: It takes roughly a month for the Moon to complete its orbit around Earth.

subtopic: Movements of the Earth (rotation and revolution)
question: In project 87, Busi described the Earth's full revolution around the Sun. How long does it take, and what does this movement, combined with its tilted axis, cause?
correct_answer: It takes 365 and a quarter days (1 year) and causes the seasons.
wrong_answers_pool: It takes 24 hours and causes day and night.
It takes 7 days and causes the tides.
It takes 24 hours and causes the seasons.
It takes 10 years and causes climate change.
It takes 1 year and causes eclipses only.
It takes 30 days and causes the phases of the Moon.
explanation: The yearly orbit combined with the tilt of the Earth's axis causes seasons.

subtopic: Systems for looking into space (telescopes)
question: Johan explained how a large optical telescope like optical telescope works to see stars (Presentation 70). What is the correct method?
correct_answer: It uses curved mirrors or lenses to gather and focus light from distant objects.
wrong_answers_pool: It uses magnets to pull the image closer.
It relies on x-rays from the sun.
It sends out radio waves and waits for an echo.
It measures the wind speed in space.
It captures sound waves from space.
It uses a giant magnifying glass only.
explanation: Optical telescopes gather visible light using precision optics.

explanation: Tidal locking means the Moon spins on its axis at the same rate it orbits Earth.

explanation: Gravity is the attractive force that keeps celestial bodies in their orbits.

subtopic: Systems for looking into space (telescopes)
question: Sarah read article 61 about the Hubble Space Telescope. Why do astronomers place such telescopes in orbit around Earth?
correct_answer: To get a clear view without the Earth's atmosphere blurring the images.
wrong_answers_pool: Because it is too cold on Earth.
Because the Moon gets in the way on Earth.
Because they need to be closer to the Sun.
Because clouds on Jupiter block the view from Earth.
Because it is cheaper to put them in space.
Because there is no gravity in space.
explanation: Space telescopes bypass the blurring and light absorption caused by Earth's atmosphere.

subtopic: Movements of the Earth (rotation and revolution)

subtopic: Movements of the Earth (rotation and revolution)
question: In project 63, Sipho described the Earth's full revolution around the Sun. How long does it take, and what does this movement, combined with its tilted axis, cause?
correct_answer: It takes 365 and a quarter days (1 year) and causes the seasons.
wrong_answers_pool: It takes 10 years and causes climate change.
It takes 24 hours and causes day and night.
It takes 365 days and causes earthquakes.
It takes 7 days and causes the tides.
It takes 1 year and causes eclipses only.
It takes 24 hours and causes the seasons.
explanation: The yearly orbit combined with the tilt of the Earth's axis causes seasons.

Because robots can live forever without power.

explanation: The yearly orbit combined with the tilt of the Earth's axis causes seasons.

correct_answer: The inner, rocky planets
explanation: The first four planets are smaller, solid, and rocky.

subtopic: Movements of the Earth (rotation and revolution)
question: In project 99, Busi described the Earth's full revolution around the Sun. How long does it take, and what does this movement, combined with its tilted axis, cause?
correct_answer: It takes 365 and a quarter days (1 year) and causes the seasons.
wrong_answers_pool: It takes 7 days and causes the tides.
It takes 24 hours and causes the seasons.
It takes 1 year and causes eclipses only.
It takes 10 years and causes climate change.
It takes 24 hours and causes day and night.
It takes 30 days and causes the phases of the Moon.
explanation: The yearly orbit combined with the tilt of the Earth's axis causes seasons.

subtopic: Systems for looking into space (telescopes)
question: Kevin read article 47 about the Hubble Space Telescope. Why do astronomers place such telescopes in orbit around Earth?
correct_answer: To get a clear view without the Earth's atmosphere blurring the images.
wrong_answers_pool: Because it is too cold on Earth.
Because it is cheaper to put them in space.
Because the Moon gets in the way on Earth.
Because they need to be closer to the Sun.
Because they would be too heavy on Earth.
Because clouds on Jupiter block the view from Earth.
explanation: Space telescopes bypass the blurring and light absorption caused by Earth's atmosphere.

subtopic: Movements of the Earth (rotation and revolution)
question: Sarah drew a diagram (Figure 10) of the Earth's rotation on its axis. What is the result of this movement?
correct_answer: It causes day and night.
wrong_answers_pool: It causes the phases of the Moon.
It makes the Sun burn hotter.
explanation: As Earth spins, the side facing the Sun has day, and the side facing away has night.

wrong_answers_pool: Because robots are faster than light.
Because Mars has no ground to stand on.

Because robots can live forever without power.

subtopic: Movements of the Moon (rotation and revolution)
question: Sipho wondered in entry 50: Why do we always see the same side of the Moon from Earth?
correct_answer: Because the Moon's rotation time is exactly the same as its revolution time around Earth.
wrong_answers_pool: Because clouds always cover the other side.
Because the Earth stops the Moon from spinning.
Because the dark side of the Moon has no light.
Because the Moon does not rotate at all.
Because the Sun's gravity locks it in place.
Because the Earth is spinning too fast.
explanation: Tidal locking means the Moon spins on its axis at the same rate it orbits Earth.

correct_answer: It is an uncrewed vehicle sent to explore other planets or moons.

subtopic: Movements of the Moon (rotation and revolution)
question: Jabu wondered in entry 54: Why do we always see the same side of the Moon from Earth?
correct_answer: Because the Moon's rotation time is exactly the same as its revolution time around Earth.
wrong_answers_pool: Because the dark side of the Moon has no light.
Because clouds always cover the other side.
Because the Moon does not rotate at all.
Because the Sun's gravity locks it in place.
Because the Earth is spinning too fast.
explanation: Tidal locking means the Moon spins on its axis at the same rate it orbits Earth.

question: Busi wondered in entry 62: Why do we always see the same side of the Moon from Earth?

Because the Moon does not rotate at all.
Because the dark side of the Moon has no light.
explanation: Tidal locking means the Moon spins on its axis at the same rate it orbits Earth.

explanation: The yearly orbit combined with the tilt of the Earth's axis causes seasons.

subtopic: The Solar System
question: For essay 90, Jabu explained why Uranus stays in its orbit around the Sun instead of flying off into space. What is the reason?
correct_answer: The strong force of gravity from the Sun pulls it inward, keeping it in orbit.

It is stuck in a giant cloud of gas.
The Moon's gravity holds it in place.
It is magnetic and sticks to the Sun.
It is pushed by the solar wind.
explanation: Gravity is the attractive force that keeps celestial bodies in their orbits.
